coredata.dc:title: Regulation on the electrical and optical properties of two-dimensional stacked graphene quantum dots/gold quantum dots/tungsten sulfide heterostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Ning
Ma, Yong
Chang, Qing
Xue, Chaorui
Zhu, Ranran
Wenjing Zheng,
Hao, Caihong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: Two-dimensional (2D) transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDCs), like WS2, have been particularly promising in the application of solar energy conversion. Nevertheless, how to enhance the absorption capacity and photogenerated dcharge transfer is yet challenging. Herein, 2D stacked heterostructures were firstly constructed by 2D Au quantum dots (QDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and WS2 monolayer. Compared with WS2 monolayer, the absorption abilities of the Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures in visible-near infrared light region of 450–2300 nm increase evidently. Simultaneously, charge redistribution occurs at the WS2–Au interfaces and generates the built-in electric field with the direction from Au QDs to the WS2 monolayer. This built-in electric field is conducive to boosting photo-induced charge separation and transfer. Notably, more obvious charge transfer and stronger built-in electric field can be achieved in WS2–Au6 interface after introducing the GQDs. Therefore, this work provides unique insights for designing new WS2-based heterostructures to improve the solar energy conversion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 2D stacked Au QDs/WS2 and GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures
First-principles calculations
Visible-near infrared light absorption
Charge transfer
Photocatalytic conversion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Atomic configurations of (a) Au3/WS2 (2# sample), (b) Au6/WS2 (3# sample), (c) Au10/WS2 (4# sample), (d) Au12/WS2 (5# sample), (e) Au16/WS2 (6# sample), (f) GQD/Au6/WS2 (7# sample) and (g) GQDs/Au16/WS2 heterostructure (8# sample); (h)–(i) Side views of GQDs/Au6/WS2 and Au6/WS2 heterostructures, respectively; (j) H max and H min values of 2#-8# samples; (k) Binding energies (E b) of 2#-8# samples.
(a)–(h) The band structures of the 1#-8# samples, respectively.
(a)–(h) The Partial density of states (PDOS) of the 1#-8# samples, respectively.

(a)–(g) Plane-average charge density difference Δρ along the z-direction of the Au QDs/WS2 and GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures (the green-yellow and light-red regions represent charge accumulation and depletion, respectively).
(a) Work functions of WS2, Au6, GQDs, Au6/WS2 and GQDs/Au6/WS2 samples; (b) Absorption spectra of the 1#-8# samples.

head.ce:title: Regulation on the electrical and optical properties of two-dimensional stacked graphene quantum dots/gold quantum dots/tungsten sulfide heterostructures

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Power Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan, 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Energy and Power Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Vehicle and Traffic Engineering, Taiyuan University of Science and Technology, Taiyuan, 030024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Vehicle and Traffic Engineering
Taiyuan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Vehicle and Traffic Engineering, Taiyuan University of Science and Technology, Taiyuan, 030024, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two-dimensional (2D) transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDCs), like WS2, have been particularly promising in the application of solar energy conversion. Nevertheless, how to enhance the absorption capacity and photogenerated dcharge transfer is yet challenging. Herein, 2D stacked heterostructures were firstly constructed by 2D Au quantum dots (QDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and WS2 monolayer. Compared with WS2 monolayer, the absorption abilities of the Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures in visible-near infrared light region of 450–2300 nm increase evidently. Simultaneously, charge redistribution occurs at the WS2–Au interfaces and generates the built-in electric field with the direction from Au QDs to the WS2 monolayer. This built-in electric field is conducive to boosting photo-induced charge separation and transfer. Notably, more obvious charge transfer and stronger built-in electric field can be achieved in WS2–Au6 interface after introducing the GQDs. Therefore, this work provides unique insights for designing new WS2-based heterostructures to improve the solar energy conversion.

ce:keyword.ce:text: 2D stacked Au QDs/WS2 and GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Two-dimensional (2D) MX2 (M = W, Mo; X = S, Se), as the most essential class of transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDCs), have been exceptionally promising building blocks for the catalyst design of photocatalytic degradation, photocatalytic conversion of molecules, photocatalytic hydrogen evolution applications [1–11], which is owing to the advantages of huge specific surface area, strong visible light absorption, short migration distance of photo-generated charge carrier and large amounts of active edge sites [10,11]. Nevertheless, for one thing, even if a theoretical investigation has proved that photocatalytic conversion like producing hydrogen can achieve by using near infrared (NIR) light [12], 2D MX2 with a few layers possesses almost no near infrared (NIR) light absorption capacity, and only less than ca. 50% of solar light can be exploited in photochemical conversion [3]. Simple functionalization such as elemental doping has been adopted to harvest more solar light but may lead to the electron scattering and recombination of photo-generated electron–hole pairs [13]. For another thing, there are fewer electrons or hole in the 2D MX2 available for the photochemical conversion, and the faster kinetics (10–100 ns) for charge recombination is disadvantageous to the photo-induced charge separation and transportation [11]. Thus, how to develop visible-near infrared (Vis-NIR) light spectrum photocatalyst with high photoinduced charge separation and transfer efficiency is still challenging.

In order to capture more solar light and facilitate the photo-induced charge-transfer, hybrid heterostructures composed of different functional components can achieve complementary advantages in performances while overcoming the drawbacks of each component. The MX2-based heterostructures include zero-dimensional (0D) metal (Au, Ag, Pt or other metal), carbon dots (carbon quantum dots (CQDs) or graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs)), CdS, C3N4, TiO2, TMDCs materials, and so on [1,3,5,7,9–11,13–18]. Among the rest, low-dimensional nanomaterials have advantages in promoting the light harvest and charge transfer. For example, carbon dots as un-conversion materials display the ability to absorb visible even NIR light [3,19]. Meanwhile, the effective transfer of photo-excited electrons from the GQDs to the MoS2 monolayers has been proved in experiment and calculation, respectively [14,20]. Notably, the optical and electrical properties of low-dimensional nanomaterials are dependent on the size and shape, which would affect the photoactivity. The CdSe quantum dots could also harvest the Vis-NIR light when their size is below ∼5 nm [21]. More intriguingly, in the low-dimensional nanomaterials, noble metals possess continuous energy levels and discrete energy levels for nanoparticles (<100 nm) and nanoclusters (<2 nm), respectively [22,23]. Noble metal nanoparticles can enhance the absorption of visible light and generate hot electrons transferred to other compounds due to the local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) [11,24,25], which significantly improves overall photo-conversion efficiency. With further reducing the size, noble metal nanoclusters like gold (Au) nanoclusters without LSPR show the molecular-like behavior, and these nanoclusters with various sizes have distinct HOMO-LUMO gaps and highest occupied orbital energies such as −5.51eV (Au6), −5.11 eV (Au38), etc. [26]. Actually, the adsorption of Aun clusters (small size, n = 1–4) on MoS2 monolayers by Au–S bonds have been constructed, and the structural and electronic properties have been investigated [27,28]. Nevertheless, the tuning effects of the metal nanoclusters with big size and discrete energy levels on the band structure, absorption and charge transfer of 2D MX2 still remain unclear, especially for the 2D metal nanoclusters (like 2D Au quantum dots (Au QDs) with monolayered atoms) and 2D MX2 in view of the strong interfacial interactions and the short migration distance of charge carriers from 2D materials interior to the interface/surface.

Herein, based on density-functional theory (DFT), we choose 2D Au QDs as metal nanoclusters, WS2 monolayers and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to firstly construct 2D stacked Au QDs/WS2 and GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures. The electrical and optical properties of 2D Au QDs/WS2 and GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures have been systematically investigated by first-principles calculation. Specifically, these heterostructures not only are energy favorable, but also possess better absorption abilities in Vis-NIR light region of 450–2300 nm and high potential in the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers. Simultaneously, the above properties can be regulated by changing the size of 2D Au QDs and the introduction of GQDs. Thus, this work would open a promising approach to improve Vis-NIR light absorption and inhibit photoinduced carrier recombination, and finally promote the improvement of the photocatalytic performance.

The calculations were done with DFT as implemented in CASTEP module [14,29–31]. One of the most important exchange-correlation potentials, the local density approximation (LDA), was adopted in the calculations, which has been demonstrated reasonably in describing the interfacial interaction between the molecules and 2D layered materials [32–34]. The plane-wave cutoff energy was set to be 480 eV, and valence electron configurations are W 5 d4 6s2, S 3s2 3p4, Au 5 d10 5s1, C 2s2 2p2 and H 1s1 electrons. The k point of 2D Au QDs and GQDs were both at gramma point for Brillouin zone integration, and the mesh of Monkhorst-Pack k-point of 2D Au QDs/WS2 and GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures was sampled with a separation of 0.017 Å−1. All the geometry structures were optimized until the self-consistent convergence and force of energy were below 0.01 eV Å−1 and 10−5 eV, respectively. We performed ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations on the representational Au QDs/WS2 and GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures within the temperature controlled at 300 K to evaluate the stability. Moreover, a large vacuum spacing of 18 Å along c-axis orientation was adopted to avoid spurious interaction between isolated WS2 monolayers, 2D Au QDs/WS2 or GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures. Meanwhile, the vacuum layers beyond 10 Å in all three directions were added for 2D Au QDs and GQDs.

Firstly, as shown in Figs. S1a–1g and Fig. 1 a–g, the pure WS2 monolayer (7 × 7 × 1 supercell containing 147 atoms), GQDs (zigzag-edged C24H12 structure), 2D Au QDs (Aun, n = 3, 6, 10, 12, 16), 2D stacked Au QDs/WS2 heterostructure and GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructure were constructed. The WS2 monolayer, Au3, 6, 10, 12, 16/WS2, GQDs/Au6, 16/WS2 heterostructures have been named as 1#-8# samples, respectively. After geometry optimization, the optimized lattice constants of the WS2 monolayer (7 × 7 × 1 supercell) were a = b = 22.07 Å, c = 21.14 Å, and α = β = 90°, γ = 120°, which is consistent with the previous results [34,35]. Subsequently, side views of Au QDs/WS2 and GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures are displayed in Fig. S1h-1l and Fig. 1h–i. It can be found that the two-dimensional structures of the Au QDs in 3#-6# samples are deformed, the edge Au atoms tend to be close to the WS2 surface. The distance between WS2 surface and 2D Au QDs was set as h (h max represents the distance between the farthest Au atom and upper S atoms, and h min refers to the distance between the nearest Au atom and upper S atoms). As illustrated in Fig. 1j, the difference values (h max-h min) of Au12/WS2 and Au16/WS2 heterostructures are less than the Au6/WS2 and Au10/WS2 samples. Moreover, the difference values further reduce after constructing three-component GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures, indicating that the introduction of the GQDs protects the two-dimensional structure of the Au QDs. In order to further analyze the stability of the heterostructure, the binding energies (E b, per atom) of Au QDs/WS2 and GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 interfaces are calculated by the following expression [14,30]:
(1)
E b
=
E
A u
Q D s / W
S 2
−
E
A u
Q D s
−
E
W
S 2
(2)
E b
=
E
G Q D s / A u
Q D s / W
S 2
−
E
G Q D s / A u
Q D s
−
E
W
S 2
Where
E
A u
Q D s / W
S 2
,
E
G Q D s / A u
Q D s / W
S 2
,
E
W
S 2
,
E
G Q D s / A u
Q D s
and
E
A u
Q D s
are the total energies of the Au QDs/WS2 and GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures, WS2 monolayer, GQDs/Au QDs and Au QDs, respectively. E b values of 2#-8# samples are shown in Fig. 1k. As seen, all E b (per atom) values are negative, indicating that the 2#-8# samples are energy favorable. Meanwhile, Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures become increasingly stable owing to the more negative E b values with the increment of the size of Au QDs. Notably, GQDs/Au6/WS2 and GQDs/Au16/WS2 heterostructures are steadier than Au6/WS2 and Au16/WS2 heterostructures, respectively. This phenomenon demonstrates that the 2D stacked GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 structure is conducive to stabilize the 2D Au QDs and construct the optimal heterostructures. Additionally, In order to further evaluate the thermal stability, the AIMD simulations were performed in Au6/WS2, GQDs/Au6/WS2, Au16/WS2 and GQDs/Au16/WS2 heterostructures within the temperature controlled at 300 K. The total simulation steps at 300 K are 5000 with a time step of 1 fs. The energy fluctuations with time during the simulations are plotted in Figs. S2a–2d. Obviously, the energy fluctuations range within a small energy window for all structures after 5000 steps, indicating that stable structures are obtained at the end of our simulations.

After analyzing the structural properties of Au QDs/WS2 and GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures, the influences of the Au QDs (GQDs/Au QDs) on the electrical properties of these heterostructures have been also investigated. First of all, the density of states (DOS) of the GQDs and Au QDs have been calculated. As shown in Fig. S3, DOS of GQDs and Au QDs are both discrete due to the quantum confinement. The energy gap of GQDs between the highest occupied states and the lowest unoccupied states is 2.92 eV (Fig. S3a), which agrees well with the calculation result (2.9 eV [36]). In addition, for 2D Au QDs, the energy gaps of Au3, Au6, Au10, Au12 and Au16 show a decreasing characteristic and are 2.88 eV, 2.02 eV, 1.27 eV, 0.96 eV and 0.56 eV (Fig. S 3b-3f).

Subsequently, the band structures and the partial density of states (PDOS) of WS2 monolayer are displayed in Figs. 2a and 3 a, respectively. As seen, the valence bands from −5 eV to 0 eV and the conduction bands from 1.5 eV to 3 eV are essentially dominated by hybridization of W 5 d and S 3p orbitals, and the direct band gap of WS2 monolayer is 1.93 eV. This value is consistent with the values acquired by LDA functional (1.94 eV [35]) and experimental methods (1.98 eV [37]). After combing with 2D Au QDs, large changes take place in the band structure and PDOS (Fig. 2b–f, Fig. 3b–f). For one thing, the whole energy levels of Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures move downward comparing with these of WS2 monolayer (Fig. 2b–f). Moreover, there are many energy levels below the Fermi level and in the forbidden band. These energy levels of Au10, 12, 16/WS2 increase in contrast to those of Au3, 6/WS2 heterostructure, which results in the reduction of the energy gap between the highest occupied states and the lowest unoccupied states (E g HL). E g HL values of Au3, 6, 10,12, 16/WS2 heterostructures are 0.28 eV, 1.58 eV, 0.92 eV, 0.93 eV and 0.40 eV, respectively. It is worth noting that the introduction of 2D Au6 clusters in the surface of WS2 monolayer merely generates one energy level below the Fermi level. For another thing, as shown in Fig. 3b–f, the lower valence bands from −5 eV to −0.5 eV are mainly hybridized from W 5 d, S 3p and Au 5 d orbitals, including a few Au 5s orbital. The conduction bands of Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures are predominantly contributed by W 5 d, S 3p and Au 5s orbitals. These phenomena indicate the electronic interactions between the Au QDs and WS2 monolayer. Interestingly, the energy levels just below the Fermi level originates mostly from Au 5s, Au 5 d, W5d and S 3p hybrid orbitals, while the energy levels in the forbidden band mainly consist of Au 5s and Au 5 d orbitals. The band structures and PDOS of GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures are illustrated in Fig. 2g–h and Fig. 3g–h, respectively. In comparison with 3# and 6# samples, more energy levels derived from the hybridized W 5 d, S 3p, Au 5s, Au 5 d and C 2p orbitals appear between the valence band maximum (VBM) and the highest occupied orbital (Fig. 2g–h, 3 g-3h), which indicates that more electrons arise in this excitable region. Simultaneously, the amount of the energy levels in the forbidden band of GQDs/Au16/WS2 heterostructure also increase, while there are also no energy levels in this area of GQDs/Au6/WS2 heterostructure.

To further elucidate the electrical characteristic of the Au QDs/WS2 interfaces, the plane-averaged charge density difference Δρ along the z-direction and 3D charge density difference are depicted in Fig. 4 and Fig. S4, respectively. The plane-averaged charge density difference can be calculated as follows [14,30,38,39]:
(3)
Δ ρ =
ρ
A u Q D s / W
S 2
−
ρ
A u Q D s
−
ρ
W
S 2
(4)
Δ ρ =
ρ
G Q D s / A u Q D s / W
S 2
−
ρ
G Q D s / A u Q D s
−
ρ
W
S 2
Where
ρ
A u QDs / W
S 2
,
ρ
G Q D s / A u QDs / W
S 2
,
ρ
A u QDs
,
ρ
W
S 2
,
ρ GQDs
,
ρ
G Q D s / A u QDs
are the charge densities of the Au QDs/WS2 and GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures, Au QDs, WS2 monolayer, GQDs and GQDs/Au QDs sets, respectively. The calculation of 3D charge density difference is in some way analogous to the plane-averaged charge density difference, which is plotted out for comparison. It is shown that the charge accumulation was displayed in green-yellow region, and the charge depletion was illustrated in light-red region (Fig. 4 and Fig. S4). The electrons mainly transfer from Au QDs to WS2 monolayers in the WS2–Au interfaces, where the electrons depletion primarily happen in the Au QDs side and the electrons accumulate in the WS2 side [38,39]. This phenomenon indicates that the charge redistribution generates the built-in electric field in the WS2–Au interfaces, and the electric field direction is from the Au QDs to the WS2 monolayer, which is conducive to boost photo-induced charge separation and transportation [38]. Additionally, the larger the wave crests of charge accumulation region in the WS2–Au interfaces are, the more obvious the charge transfer is, and the stronger the interfacial interaction is. As shown in Fig. 4a–e, the charge aggregation and depletion peaks in the WS2–Au10, 12, 16 interfaces are apparently higher than the WS2–Au3, 6 interfaces, which illustrates the more robust charge transfer and interfacial interaction of the former. Notably, these peaks of WS2–Au16 interface reduce slightly, whereas WS2–Au6 interface increase markedly after introducing the GQDs above the Au6 clusters (Figs. S4f and S4g), demonstrating the hugest improvement in the charge transfer and interaction of WS2–Au6 (GQDs) interface. Besides, the amount of charge transfer is also significant for describing the interaction between the Au clusters and WS2 monolayer [40]. As displayed in Fig. S5, the amount of charge transfer increases gradually with the increment of Aun size, and the direction is from Au QDs to WS2. Meanwhile, compared with Au6/WS2 and Au16/WS2 heterostructures, the amounts of the charge transfer both improve after the introduction of GQDs. The above results are consistent with the plane-averaged charge density difference.

To further explore the above physical phenomena, the work function of WS2 monolayer, Au6 clusters, GQDs, Au6/WS2 and GQDs/Au6/WS2 heterostructures were calculated to investigate the charge transfer of the WS2–Au6 interfaces, as displayed in Fig. 5 a. The work function was calculated by the following expression [38]:
(5)
Φ =
E
V A C
−
E F
where
E
V A C
and
E F
are the electrostatic potential in the vacuum position and Fermi energy, respectively. The work functions of WS2, Au6 and GQDs are 5.91, 5.42 and 5.06 eV, respectively. The Fermi levels tend to be balanced When WS2 monolayer and Au6 cluster contact with each other, and then the work function of Au6/WS2 heterostructure turns into 5.67 eV. In this process, electrons would migrate from Au6 clusters to WS2 surface, resulting in the increment of the electron density in WS2 surface and electrons depletion in Au6 cluster. Based on this fact, the built-in electric field has been formed and the direction is from Au6 to WS2, which is consistent with the results of plane-averaged charge density difference and 3D charge density difference. Additionally, the work function of GQDs/Au6/WS2 heterostructure become 5.19 eV after contacting. The electrons transfer from GQDs to Au6 cluster, then more electrons of Au6 surface will shift to WS2 monolayer, which leads to the enhancement of the interfacial charge transfer and built-in electric field in the Au6-WS2 interface. When the Au6/WS2 and GQDs/Au6/WS2 heterostructures are was excited by solar light, the photogenerated electrons of WS2 would migrate to Au6 cluster driven by the built-in electric field in the Au6-WS2 interface.

It is well known that the strong absorption ability can make the semiconductor generate more photo-induced charges, which is momentous for acquiring high solar energy conversion efficiency [3,9–12]. Sang et al. [3] has synthesized WS2 nanosheets with the valuable full solar-spectrum absorption ability. Surprisingly, this WS2 nanosheets possess high NIR light photocatalytic activity. Nevertheless, WS2 monolayer merely has strong UV and visible light absorptive and photocatalytic activity. How to improve the near infrared light absorption is urgent. Fig. 5b shows the optical absorption of the WS2 monolayer, Au QDs/WS2, GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures, which can be calculation from the following equation [14,39,41]:
(6)
α =
(
2
ω
)
[
ε 1
( ω )
2
+
ε 2
( ω )
2
−
ε 1
( ω )
]
1 2
where ω,
ε 2
( ω )
and
ε 1
( ω )
are the light frequency, imaginary and real parts of the dielectric function. The calculated
ε 1
( ω )
and
ε 2
( ω )
of dielectric function versus energy of the 1#-8# samples are illustrated in Fig. S6. As illustrated in Fig. 5b, there is better absorption in the range of 300–900 nm for the WS2 monolayers, especially in the range of visible light. It is not difficult to find that there is hardly any light absorption in the NIR region of 900–2300 nm owing to the high band gap (about ∼1.93 eV) and absorption edge. By contrast, the absorption intensities of Au QDs/WS2 and GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructure in the vis-NIR region of 450–2300 nm increase remarkably. Notably, the absorption intensities in the above region increase evidently with the increment of the Au QDs size. Meanwhile, the absorption abilities of GQDs/Au6, 16/WS2 heterostructures are also comparable to those of Au6, 16/WS2 heterostructures, respectively. Therefore, the construction of 2D stacked Au QDs/WS2 and GQD/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures does improve the ability absorbing more solar light, which can be used for designing efficient Vis-NIR photocatalyst.

In summary, 2D stacked Au QDs/WS2 and GQD/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures were constructed by 2D Au3, 6, 10, 12, 16 QDs, GQDs (C24H12) and WS2 monolayer. These heterostructures have negative binding energies, indicating that they are energy favorable. Additionally, the heterostructures introduced by GQDs or with higher Au QDs sizes become more stable. Then the electronic and optical properties of GQDs, Au QDs, WS2 and the stacked heterostructures have been investigated. Results display that the electronic properties of GQDs, Au QDs and WS2 monolayers coincide with the earlier studies. There are many energy levels below the Fermi level and in the forbidden band area, which is fallen under the hybridization effect originated from the Au 5s, Au 5 d orbitals for Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures and the Au 5s, Au 5 d, C2p orbitals for GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures. Meanwhile, the charges transfer from Au QDs to WS2 monolayers in the WS2–Au interfaces where generates the built-in electric field with the direction from Au QDs to the WS2 monolayer，which is beneficial to the photo-induced charge separation and transfer. Interestingly, more robust charge transfer and interfacial interaction in WS2–Au6 interface after introducing the GQDs can lead to better effect on the photocatalytic performance. Additionally, compared with the WS2 monolayer, Au QDs/GQDs and GQDs/Au QDs/WS2 heterostructures has high absorption in visible-near infrared light region of 450–2300 nm, which makes for absorbing more solar light. Therefore, the innovative construction of 2D stacked heterostructures enhance the Vis-NIR light absorption and promote the charge transfer, finally realize the noteworthy enhancement of the photocatalytic performance.

Ning Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Resubmission. Yong Ma, Qing Chang, Chaorui Xue: Software, Investigation. Ranran Zhu: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Resubmission. Wenjing Zheng, Caihong Hao: Data curation, Validation. Shengliang Hu: Conceptualization, Writing- Reviewing and Editing, Supervision. All authors take part in discussion on conclusion.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (nos. 22105181, 22202186); Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (20210302123029, 20210302124127, 202103021223183, 202203021221100); Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (2020L0279, 2020L0293), China; Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, China; Program for the Innovative Talents of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi, China; and Transformation of Scientific and Technological Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (TSTAP), China. North University of China Fund for “333” talent project research.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.physb.2023.414884.
